summary: Bryan Cantrill responds to Oakland A's owner John Fisher's tone-deaf claim that the team's relocation to Las Vegas has been 'worse for me than for you.' Cantrill systematically dismantles this claim by cataloging the real harm to fans, employees, and small businesses, but then pivots to argue there's an ironic truth to Fisher's words: the shared community, joy, and humanity that sports create are things Fisher will never possess. The essay uses the extraordinary Reverse Boycott game of June 2023 as proof that Oakland fans have something money and inheritance cannot buy.
key_points: John Fisher's claim that the A's relocation is worse for him than fans is both tone-deaf and revealing
The Reverse Boycott game demonstrated the deep communal power of sports fandom
Fisher's incompetence as an owner stems from treating a team as mere inherited property rather than a community institution
Sports bind people across generations and circumstances in ways that transcend what happens on the field
Oakland fans retain their solidarity and community even as the team is taken from them
Fisher's legacy will be defined by destruction rather than creation
money_quotes: Sports bring us together – they bind us across generation, disposition, and circumstance. A family that might agree on little else may shout in indignant agreement that that wasn't pass interference or that he was obviously safe.
In short, sports are the raw id of our own humanity.

It would be tempting to say that he instead sees sports as only a business, but even this gives him too much credit: the only business he knows is assuring the mechanics of inheritance – of hoarding the spoils of his birth.
In John Fisher's obituary, it will not speak of what he built, but of what he broke; not of what he gave, but of what he took away.
He is now MLB's famous failson, the Connor Roy of professional sports.

We Oakland A's fans will always have what cannot be taken away: our solidarity with – and love for – one another. We are raucous, brainy, creative, and eclectic; our lives are richer for having one another in them.
key_insight: The deepest cost of Fisher's betrayal falls on Fisher himself, because the communal bonds and shared humanity that make sports meaningful are things his inherited wealth and managerial incompetence can never purchase.
